speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three, interview with Mr. Zoltan Wieder. We were talking-- or you were telling us about the restrictions, and the new laws that were coming in, and how they were affecting you. What other laws came in that affected you personally, or that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] actually, the later laws which came in when we had to where the star, the yellow star. But that was in '44, when the Germans came into Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that, you didn't where any?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before that, different-- the laws, there were anti-Jewish laws. Like, I wasn't-- because I-- the business people, actually, were affected much more. And-- and-- but I was affected as anti-Semitism, and calling you names, and trying to hit you, or whatever, because you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: The synagogue, and the [INAUDIBLE], the rabbis, were there any attempts to unify, or to alert the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbi-- we had a rabbi there, his first rabbi. His name Mayer Wolf Nadler. And he was an older rabbi. And when I was a young child, I know I went to him to study with him also. And they lived only a few-- two blocks-- I mean, two houses, actually, from our house. And he had a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a grandson, which was my age. So we were very good friends. And so he was-- he-- he tried, always, to unify the [INAUDIBLE]. And it, really, it was-- everybody was-- they were all friends. And there were never discussions, or I mean fights or something in the [INAUDIBLE]. And then when he died-- I remember I was a young child when he died. And he was probably 62 or something. And they hired another rabbi from Cluj. His name was [? Stroli, ?] Rabbi [? Stroli, ?] a very nice younger man with a wife and two children, young children. And I know I studied with him too. So, you know, I usually went in the morning at 6:00 o'clock to study with him, or in the evening late, or shabbat, or some other free times. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] actually, my brother Adolph, he was-- he went to four years to-- to learn in the Sheba in, in Cluj. And he got-- he got, actually, the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as a rabbi. He became a rabbi, actually. He didn't work as a rabbi, but he had the title.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the-- were there any rules about taking people to work at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were in the school. They took us once a week. They called it [NON-ENGLISH]. And we did some work. But not-- we did some work once a week, different type of--

speaker: interviewer
text: For the Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the Hungarian, cleaning parks or something, not-- nothing special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the Jewish children, but that was later on. And especially in the winter, they took, like I said, to snow shoveling-- Jewish people, just Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after '40? Yeah, in '40, I was 15 years old, actually, in 1940 when the Hungarians came in. I was actually not 15 even. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when the Germans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the Germans came in on March 22nd, 1944. And the next day, some of the army units came to our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: German army?

speaker: interviewee
text: German army units, they went to Budapest first. And the next day, they were in our town, some of the units. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Hungarian authorities-- because in 1940, when they came in, they put all Hungarians to the city, to the police-- the mayor, the police captain, everybody, all Hungarians. They brought Hungarians forms main Hungary. And all the Romanians, they threw out. And-- and-- when they came in, the Germans, the next day, the mayor-- and I mean the police-- they put some of the German officers to Jewish homes-- into Jewish homes. And our home too, we had to gave up one room, and let in a German officer. I remember I was talking to him in German. And he was telling me-- he was from Hamburg. And he was telling me, if we lose the war, I don't want to live anymore. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were very afraid. My sister was two years younger than me. But she was a beautiful tall girl, nice girl. So we took her right away-- right away to my aunt's home. And she was sleeping there, and staying there all the time. We were afraid. And he-- he was in our home there, in a room. And the next--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he eat with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: He just slept there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just slept there, slept there. The whole day he was away, came home in the evening. No, he didn't eat with us. And he just slept there. But he didn't speak to nobody else. To me, sometimes he called me, and he talked to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he tell you about, other than if the war was lost?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was just telling me about the war. They will win the war. If they don't win, he doesn't want to live. And he didn't tell me nothing else. And he said he was a pharmacist from Hamburg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he had-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] then in about a week, they came out with a law we have to wear the stars-- stars of the-- yellow stars, everybody, my sisters, and myself. My brother wasn't home. My brother, because he was of military age, they took him to a labor camp. The Hungarian, they didn't take to military training and to regular training. They took to a labor camp, the military age Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Hungarian Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarians, the Jews, yeah, they took them. So my brother, he was 21. So they took him to a labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was when the Germans came in or before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, before. He was already-- he was-- in '42, he was 21 years old. So they took him to, in '42 already, to the labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had contact. And I remember he didn't-- you know, we ate kosher at that time. And it was a big deal not to eat kosher. And we tied to send him-- my mother sent him every week by mail, sent him a package with food. And he tried not to eat non-kosher there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in a labor camp between Satu Mare and [INAUDIBLE], which was [INAUDIBLE]. They call it [INAUDIBLE] in Romanian, [INAUDIBLE] in Hungarian. And we wrote letters. We wrote them back. And, actually, my mother, she was terrific. She was writing letters to everybody in the family all around the country. And she could write 10, 12 pages in half an hour. And she was keeping the contact with everybody. And so my brother wasn't home. He was in the labor camp at the time. So we had to wear the stars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the stars?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, the stars we had to buy. They had already some stores there. They buy yellow stars. And my mother, she put it on. We had to sew it on to the clothes. You couldn't go out on the street without the yellow star. You had it on the left side of your jacket. Or if you wore a shirt, you have to on the shirt. You just couldn't-- if they find you without that, they put you in jail. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then they-- we had the-- yeah, it was actually before-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my uncle, that was before. In '40, when they came into Hungarian, two weeks later they put him into jail for a few weeks, four weeks or so. So they had all kinds of things with the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a yellow star. And at that time, we were restricted already. We couldn't leave the town. They said that time, you can't leave the town. And-- and you have to wear the star all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was about from April-- beginning of April, we wear the yellow stars. And then two, three weeks after that, about three weeks after that, in one early morning, they came to our house, one detective-- Hungarian detective, which was part of the authorities there who came from main Hungary, and two policemen. And they brought with them some Hungarian Levente. They were the young Hungarians who they used for this to help them with the deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say they were called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Levente.

speaker: interviewer
text: Levente.

speaker: interviewee
text: Levente, like here, the scout-- the scout boys, or the Boy Scouts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Boy Scouts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Boy Scouts.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-E-V-E-N-T-E, t as in Thomas, E, Levente. They called them Leventes, like Boy Scouts. But they were like 15, 16, 17 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were they supposed to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They supposed to make sure everybody is caught up with them, and taken out. So they came about 6:00, or 5:30 in the morning. And the first thing this guy, the detective, gave me a-- hit me on the face. And so they gave us half an hour to put together some food and some-- but, initially, they-- we prepared already, because they said they will-- we heard some rumors they were taking away us, they were taking away. So my mother prepared food, and all kinds of foods, and cakes. And we had big bags prepared with all kind of clothing. And I remember we had a neighbor next to our house. We left her some-- my mother talked to them some things, linen and something put away for us, and whatever. So they came in that morning. They gave us half an hour or an hour. So we had to get together the things. And they took us from the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hide anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you. I had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] meet my friend who was that Rabbi Nadler's grandson. We talked about-- we said, maybe we should put some money, hide some money with us. We don't know where we are going. Maybe we-- and, actually, we had some pengo. That were the Hungarian money at the time. We had some money, because my mother sold-- she had some from her father some property, and sold it about six months earlier. In [INAUDIBLE] there, in that town, and some fields, some property.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she-- we got some money. And so we said, let's hide something. And how we are going to hide it? So we put some-- and I had a cap. We opened the cap here in the front, and put in some of the hundreds pengos. They called it pengo, that money. Paper money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paper money, pengos, hundreds, hundreds of pengos. And we hide some in a brush. We opened the brush. And inside the brush, the top of the brush we took it. And we put in-- we made a, like a hole, a big hole, put in some paper. So I had that cap on myself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we had some-- my mother had some jewelry. We didn't have too much, a little bit. My father had a golden watch, I remember. Like, they had a pocket watch. And my mother had the earrings, what she got when she got married, and a ring. So we hide it there in my grandfather's yard someplace. But other things-- she had the ring, the other ring on, and whatever she had with her. And then they took us out. And-- and we had to carry the big bags on our-- and they took us to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take any dishes or things like that, eating utensils?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- yeah, I think we took-- we took one or two dishes, and some forks and knives, spoons, Yeah, we took a few. And we had some food. She prepared all kind of food. And they took us to the synagogue. And they took every Jew the same morning. They were all the places, covered all the places in the city. They took everybody to the synagogue, crowded there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: The synagogue was only very close to my house.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we walked, sure we walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who guarded you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They guarded us, the detective, and the police, and the leventes, these guys, make sure we are not running away or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody watch you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarians, yeah, they were watching as we go, and looking around. They were laughing, some of them. So we went to-- it was a small street near our street. They called it the synagogue street. That was the name. And we went to the synagogue. And the whole town, 300 people-- less than 300, because some people they were in the labor camps already. And they were on the floor there, and in the benches there. And we were there for three days, day night there. And they were guarded us outside. You couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside, we prayed, we talked. We didn't know. They were going around all kind of rumors. They are taking your to a labor camp here and there. We could never imagine what's going to be take us to Auschwitz, they kill us. You couldn't imagine this kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about concentration camps or anything of that sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. We didn't didn't hear about it. We didn't know nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you searched in the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: The synagogue, no, they didn't search us. So we went there. And then after three days, they put us in trucks. And they took us to [INAUDIBLE], to a-- to a camp which was actually a farm. And it was-- they had the big barns there. And they put there in the barn-- open barns. And it was in April. it was pretty cold at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] was a camp or a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a ghetto, actually, a ghetto. But we were there only about two weeks. I remember at that time-- there already, they cut our hairs. And-- and they put a stripe in the middle of the head. So you should be recognized if you try to escape or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarian did that already, the gendarmes, the Hungarian gendarmes. They call that was the military arm that-- they were very cruel, the Hungarian gendarmes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The women too?

speaker: interviewee
text: The women too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you searched in that ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the women, they were not. The women, they let the hairs. No, no, sorry. The women were in Auschwitz later, but the man. No, we were not searched. And so we were there. And they didn't give us any food. So what we ate what we had. We made a little-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we tried to get some wood, made fire. And we warmed up something to eat, whatever we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said they were barns. They put you into barns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the barns, animals barns.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many barns were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were there, I think, about six big barns, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any fence around?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, fence, nothing. Oh, you mean around the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, around that farm, it was a fence. It was guard there, Hungarian gendarmes. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the spokesman for the Jewish community? Was there a [NON-ENGLISH]? Or were there other spokesman? There-- there we didn't have actually-- we had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember, they put-- from the Jews, they put some policeman. They made policemen some of them to watch, and to be in contact, and watch the people. And there, actually, we didn't have any spokesman or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there, after probably 10 days or 12 days, it was up to two weeks. And I remember, I was-- twice, German officers came in to check out what's happening. They came in to there. And once, I went out to get some water some place. And they let me out with somebody. They took me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was thinking-- and I was in there in a place. I got some water. And I didn't see nobody near me. I could have escaped. But who knew what? I didn't-- I mean, they would have find me, probably. But I had my family there. So-- What did people do all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: They put us some-- I think we did some-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some work there, some roads. We did some work there. I'm just remembering, we did something there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But mostly, not too much. We tried to-- we busy with cooking something, you know? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Walking around, talking, and-- and-- crying a lot. And we did-- they give us also something to-- we check-- we do some work there in the-- in the ghetto there-- roads, or something, or fix something there, whatever. They didn't provide any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't provide any food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone have special privileges there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the rabbis treated differently? Was the rabbi treated differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the same way, same way, not differently. And from there, after two weeks, they put us in trucks. And it took us to the ghetto in Baia-Mare, which was the bigger ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was near the-- there was a big factory there, chemical factory. They called it [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. And the ghetto was right near that chemical factory, on a big piece of land there. And there, there were some camp-- some barracks there already, but open barracks. And-- and we tried to make some camps there. And we were there about-- in there, we had-- they were going out all kind of rumors. And they spread the rumors, actually, the Hungarian authorities, the German and Hungarian authorities. They spread rumors there that we are going to be taken to mainland Hungary, to the main part of Hungary, to some labor camps until the war is over, and all kinds of rumors like that. And I find out later, some families-- a few families in Baia-Mare, they poisoned themselves before they took the them into the ghetto, I think three or four families. So probably, they were-- maybe they knew something already. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew the families?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard about them. I didn't know them personally actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto we were-- in the ghetto, we had-- we had an open barrack there. And we had the bunk bed or something there, or on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother or father were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother and father, and the two sisters. My brother, no, because he was in the labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you all work?

speaker: interviewee
text: And yeah, we did some-- some work there, with the roads, or with the camp doing something. And tied to-- we got some potatoes there, I remember. I don't know how-- what-- we cooked some potatoes. And we try-- made some fire there. And I think we were there only about two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there children in this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, all the children were there in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did people do with their children? Did they organize any activities for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were no activities.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left your home, did you take your talllit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I took my [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't have tallit because I was a young boy. I didn't need a tallit. You know, you don't have tallit there until you-- until you marry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, not 13.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not 13-- no, you got the tallit only when you marry. But my father had his tallit. And I had my [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any organized ritual in any of these ghettos?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember any organized ritual. Everybody tried to daven in his place there when he can. But I don't remember any organized, like a or something. No, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever sick in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wasn't. Thank God, I wasn't. No, I wasn't sick. Nobody was sick in the Ghetto

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody die in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people did, they did die, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took them out? Or were they-- were you able to have a funeral of any sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I don't remember. I don't remember. I know there were some people died. But I don't remember what-- I know they took them out. I don't think so there were a funeral. They didn't allow something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about wedding, anybody get married there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any babies born there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not as I know. No babies were born there. We were there about-- I think about three weeks in that ghetto. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anyone punished in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't remember seeing something like this. I remember when we went to the trains, to the cattle trains, they were hit in the back. They were pushed from the gendarmes. I had also a big bag on my head-- on my back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was pushed, and pushed, and scream, yelled, you know? Did you still have your own clothes and stuff?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the clothes which I got in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, the clothes when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the clothes, yeah, I had my own clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And And what about the money you had taken with you? Did you have it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That money, it was on my hat. And that was with me. The hat was on my head. And that brush was in the bag. And they put us in cattle cars, about 100, 120 people to a cattle car. And they pushed us in. And they closed the wagons. And we didn't-- they gave us a little water once in a while. There were no facilities. They just had a bucket there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother, my father, my sisters, my uncle, my two uncles, my two aunts, four cousins. That was from immediate my family there-- oh, and another aunt with a daughter. That aunt had a sick daughter. When she was young, three years old, she became sick from [? scarlatina. ?] And then they didn't have medicine this. And she became hunchback. She had a hunchback. And she was only about 22 at that time, or 21 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had no idea, no idea. But in the trains, people were already sick, some people hungry. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and, you know, together so many people. And people were already so exhausted, and so tired. And when we got-- we got to-- I remember to a station, Kassa, which is a city today in Slovakia. At that time, it was Hungary. And the train stopped there. And the Hungarian gendarmes, they were in each cattle-- near the wagons, there were Hungarian gendarmes. They came in. And they said, you have to give up everything. If you have money or jewelry, because we are going to search you. And we are going to shot you right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to change our tape again.
